front: Poem: oldest extant Old English poem; nine lines (eighteen half-lines) praising God as creator
Poem: opens 'Now we ought to praise the Guardian of the heavenly kingdom'
Poem: composed in a dream vision after the poet left a banquet because he 'did not know how to sing'; told 'Sing me creation'
Poem: survives only in copies of Bede's Ecclesiastical History of the English People
Poem: describes God creating 'heaven as a roof' and 'middle-earth' for the 'children of earth'
Author: illiterate cow-herd at Whitby Abbey under Abbess Hild (St. Hilda)
Poem: Old English work in which the cross narrates Christ's crucifixion; preserved in the Vercelli Codex; sometimes attributed to this poet or Cynewulf
back: The Dream of the Rood (attributed to Caedmon)

front: Poem: part also carved on the eighth-century Ruthwell Cross in Scotland
back: The Dream of the Rood (attributed to Caedmon)

front: Author: a fourth major codex of Old English literature is named for this poet, though he wrote none of it
